The Places That Stay With You

lesson.menu_title: The Trip That Changed Everything -- Looking Back on What Shaped You
lesson.menu_desc: Reading and reflecting on what shaped you with mixed conditionals -- 28 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 16 -- The Trip That Changed Everything
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Marcelo de Azevedo Souza | Aula 16 -- The Trip That Changed Everything

vocab.title: Match each word to its meaning
defs: an experience that makes you realize you must change
having a very big effect on your whole life
having a strong influence on how you develop
to widen your experience and understanding
something that causes an important change
an experience that surprises you and teaches you something new
an experience that marks an important stage of life
to end up back where you started, but changed

paras: I was thirty-one, and I almost did not go. The ticket sat in my inbox for a week while I found reasons to stay: work, money, the comfort of a life I already knew. If I had listened to those reasons, I would not be the person I am today. But something made me say yes, and that one decision turned out to be the catalyst for everything that followed.

The trip itself was ordinary enough. Three weeks, one backpack, a country whose language I could not speak. And yet it was an eye-opener. I met people who had almost nothing and gave everything. I saw a way of living that broadened my horizons in a single month more than the previous ten years had. It was, without question, the most formative experience of my adult life. If I were the same cautious person I had been before, none of it would have happened.

People ask me why one short trip mattered so much. The truth is that it was a wake-up call. I came home, changed my work, and slowly built a life that looks nothing like the one I left. In a strange way, I have come full circle: I am back in the same city, but I am not the same man. If I had never boarded that plane, I would still be living a smaller life, wondering what was out there.
reading.source: Adapted from a travel memoir, The Trip That Changed Me

reading.prompt: What is the main idea of the text?
choices: The writer regrets taking a trip that wasted three weeks of his life.
One trip the writer almost skipped became the catalyst that reshaped who he is today.
The best trips are the longest and most expensive ones.

gapfill.text: This text is full of MIXED CONDITIONALS, which link a past cause to a present result, or a present truth to a past result. PAST to PRESENT: 'If I HADN'T GONE (past perfect), I WOULDN'T BE (would + base) who I am now.' PRESENT to PAST: 'If I WEREN'T (past simple) so curious, I WOULD NEVER HAVE GONE (would have + participle).' Use HAD + participle for a past condition, and WOULD HAVE + participle for a past result.

bank.label: Useful language for talking about what shaped you
items: If I hadn't taken that trip, I wouldn't be who I am today.
If I weren't so curious, I would never have gone.
That trip was the catalyst for everything that followed.
It was, without question, the most formative experience of my life.
It was a real eye-opener that broadened my horizons.
In a way, I have come full circle.
If I had stayed home, I would still be living a smaller life.
That one decision changed the course of my life.

lf.text: A and C link a PAST cause to a PRESENT result: if + past perfect (had gone), then would + base (wouldn't be). B and D, in blue, link a PRESENT truth to a PAST result: if + past simple (weren't), then would have + participle (would have gone). Choose each half by asking: is this part about the past or about now?

items: Scenario 1
Talk about a decision that shaped who you are now. Use a past cause and a present result: 'If I hadn't..., I wouldn't be...'.
Scenario 2
Explain a past action with a present quality of yours: 'If I weren't..., I would never have...'.
Scenario 3
Describe how a trip or a move changed you, mixing a past cause and a present result.
Scenario 4
Think of a road you did not take. Imagine your life now if you had chosen differently: 'If I had..., I would...'.

answerkey.title: Reveal the answer key
list: Gap-fill: 1 = hadn't taken, 2 = wouldn't be, 3 = weren't, 4 = would never have, 5 = had stayed, 6 = would still be.
Model (past to present): 'If I hadn't taken that trip, I wouldn't be who I am today.'
Model (present to past): 'If I weren't so open to new things, I would never have gone.'
Structure: past cause = if + had + participle; present result = would + base. Present truth = if + past simple (were); past result = would have + participle.
answerkey.note: Do not use 'would' in the if-clause: not 'if I would have gone' but 'if I had gone'. And use 'weren't' (not 'wasn't') for an unreal present state: 'if I weren't so cautious'.

listenings.text: Hi Marcelo, it is Freya. You asked me about the trip that changed my life, and honestly, I could talk about it for hours. I was twenty-four, stuck in a job I did not love, and a friend invited me to travel for a month. If I had said no, I would still be in that same office right now, I am sure of it. That trip was a real wake-up call. It broadened my horizons in a way nothing else ever had. I met a woman who ran a tiny school in the mountains, and watching her changed everything for me. If I weren't the kind of person who says yes to things, I would never have gone, and I would never have found the work I do now. It is funny. I have come full circle. I live a quiet life again, but I am a completely different person. That is what one trip can do. Bye now.

Welcome back to the show. Today, why some trips change us, and most do not. Here is the strange thing. You can take ten holidays and remember none of them, and then one journey rearranges your whole life. Psychologists call these formative experiences, and they tend to share a few features. They pull you out of your routine. They put you among people who live very differently. And they arrive at a moment when you are ready, even if you do not know it. Think about it this way. If you had not been at a certain point in your life, that same trip might have meant nothing. And if you were a slightly different person, you might never have gone at all. That is the quiet mathematics of a life-changing journey. The place matters less than the timing, and the timing matters less than your willingness to say yes. So the next time a door opens, remember. The trip that changes everything rarely announces itself. It just asks whether you are brave enough to walk through.
